key_insight: A fully differentiable, GPU-accelerated FEM workflow can both simulate finite-strain anisotropic plasticity and efficiently recover constitutive parameters from full-field data.
engineering_relevance: This enables faster high-fidelity mechanics simulation and practical inverse material characterization for advanced manufacturing and process design.
method: Built a JAX-based FEM framework with automatic differentiation through constitutive updates and solvers.
Parallelized elemental evaluation, sparse assembly, and sparse linear solve on GPUs.
Used topology-optimized heterogeneous specimens and displacement fields to identify anisotropic yield and hardening parameters.
evidence_signal: The abstract reports up to 9.4× speed-up on a single NVIDIA H100 GPU for a 3 million-DOF forward problem and accurate parameter recovery in multiple inverse characterization cases.
watch_item: Performance and robustness may depend on solver scalability, GPU memory limits, and the quality of full-field experimental data for complex inverse problems.
paper_title: A Differentiable GPU-Accelerated Finite Element Framework for Inverse Characterization of Finite-Strain Anisotropic Plasticity
